section.title: The Birth of Samson
section.sectionRationale: The last and longest of the judge stories opens not with a battle but with a birth. Israel has sunk again into evil, and this time God does not even wait for them to cry out; unbidden, He sends the Angel of the LORD to a barren woman in Zorah to announce a son who will be a Nazirite from the womb and begin to deliver Israel from the Philistines. The chapter is a sustained theophany, awe-filled and tender, in which the mysterious visitor whose name is Wonderful ascends in the altar flame. It is one whole movement: grace announcing a deliverer before the deliverer draws a breath.
section.thesis: Into a season so dark that Israel no longer even cries for help, God graciously takes the initiative, sending the Angel of the LORD to a barren woman to promise and set apart a deliverer from the womb; the Nazirite child is God's gift, and the awesome visitor whose name is Wonderful, worshiped in the ascending flame, unveils the God who stoops to save an undeserving people.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the Angel's announcement to the barren woman, verses 1 to 7; Manoah's prayer and the Angel's return, verses 8 to 14; the offering, the ascending flame, and the name Wonderful, verses 15 to 23; and the birth of Samson and the stirring of the Spirit, verses 24 to 25.

units.label: The Angel announces a son to the barren woman
units.note: a Nazirite to God from the womb

verses.text: Again the Israelites did evil in the sight of the LORD, so He delivered them into the hand of the Philistines for forty years.
Now there was a man from Zorah named Manoah, from the clan of the Danites, whose wife was barren and had no children.
The angel of the LORD appeared to the woman and said to her, “It is true that you are barren and have no children; but you will conceive and give birth to a son.
Now please be careful not to drink wine or strong drink, and not to eat anything unclean.

For behold, you will conceive and give birth to a son. And no razor shall touch his head, because the boy will be a {{nazir|Nazirite}} to God from the womb, and he will begin the deliverance of Israel from the hand of the Philistines.”
So the woman went and told her husband, “A man of God came to me. His appearance was like the angel of God, exceedingly awesome. I did not ask him where he came from, and he did not tell me his name.

But he said to me, ‘Behold, you will conceive and give birth to a son. Now, therefore, do not drink wine or strong drink, and do not eat anything unclean, because the boy will be a Nazirite to God from the womb until the day of his death.’”

units.label: Manoah's prayer and the Angel's return
units.note: teach us how to raise the boy

verses.text: Then Manoah prayed to the LORD, “Please, O Lord, let the man of God You sent us come to us again to teach us how to raise the boy who is to be born.”
And God listened to the voice of Manoah, and the {{malak|angel}} of God returned to the woman as she was sitting in the field; but her husband Manoah was not with her.
The woman ran quickly to tell her husband, “Behold, the man who came to me the other day has reappeared!”
So Manoah got up and followed his wife. When he came to the man, he asked, “Are you the man who spoke to my wife?” “I am,” he said.
Then Manoah asked, “When your words come to pass, what will be the boy’s rule of life and mission?”
So the angel of the LORD answered Manoah, “Your wife is to do everything I told her.
She must not eat anything that comes from the vine, nor drink any wine or strong drink, nor eat anything unclean. She must do everything I have commanded her.”

units.label: The ascending flame and the name Wonderful

verses.text: “Please stay here,” Manoah said to the angel of the LORD, “and we will prepare a young goat for you.”
And the angel of the LORD replied, “Even if I stay, I will not eat your food. But if you prepare a burnt offering, offer it to the LORD.” For Manoah did not know that it was the angel of the LORD.
Then Manoah said to the angel of the LORD, “What is your name, so that we may honor you when your word comes to pass?”
“Why do you ask my name,” said the angel of the LORD, “since it is {{pele|beyond comprehension}}?”
Then Manoah took a young goat and a grain offering and offered them on a rock to the LORD. And as Manoah and his wife looked on, the LORD did a marvelous thing.
When the flame went up from the altar to the sky, the angel of the LORD ascended in the flame. When Manoah and his wife saw this, they fell facedown to the ground.
And when the angel of the LORD did not appear again to Manoah and his wife, Manoah realized that it had been the angel of the LORD.
“We are going to die,” he said to his wife, “for we have seen God!”

But his wife replied, “If the LORD had intended to kill us, He would not have accepted the burnt offering and the grain offering from our hands, nor would He have shown us all these things or spoken to us this way.”

units.label: The birth of Samson and the stirring of the Spirit

verses.text: So the woman gave birth to a son and named him Samson. The boy grew, and the LORD blessed him.
And the {{ruach|Spirit}} of the LORD began to stir him at Mahaneh-dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol.

malak.gloss: Messenger, angel. The Angel of the LORD in this chapter is no ordinary messenger: He accepts a burnt offering, does a marvelous thing, ascends in the flame, and is finally identified with God Himself, so that Manoah cries, we have seen God. Many read this as a Christophany, an appearing of the eternal Son before His incarnation.

nazir.gloss: A Nazirite, one separated or consecrated to God, marked by not cutting the hair, avoiding wine and grape products, and shunning defilement, Numbers 6. Samson is a Nazirite from the womb until death, set apart by God before he is even conceived, a living sign that his whole life belongs to the LORD and to His saving purpose.

pele.gloss: Wonder, marvel; the root behind the Angel's answer that His name is beyond comprehension, or wonderful. The very same word names the coming Messiah in Isaiah 9:6, Wonderful Counselor. The visitor who bears an unsearchable, wonderful name and ascends in the flame reveals the God whose ways are past finding out.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, breath. The Spirit of the LORD who begins to stir Samson is the same Spirit who empowered the earlier judges, the source of the strength and courage by which God will work deliverance. That God's own Spirit moves the child marks Samson's rescue of Israel as God's work, not merely human might.

basic.context: Israel does evil once more, and God hands them to the Philistines for forty years. Strikingly, no cry for rescue is recorded; the people seem too settled in their oppression even to call out. Yet God acts anyway. The Angel of the LORD comes to a barren woman, the wife of Manoah of Dan, and promises her a son who is to be a Nazirite from the womb, set apart to begin delivering Israel. Manoah prays for the visitor to return, and He does, repeating the instructions. When they prepare an offering, the Angel does a marvelous thing and ascends in the altar flame, and the couple realize they have seen God. In time the woman bears Samson, the LORD blesses him, and the Spirit of the LORD begins to stir him.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, told with reverence and wonder, and its heart is God's gracious initiative. We should not reduce it to a lesson on parenting or turn the mysterious visitor into a mere angel; the text presses us toward awe, and toward the God who stoops to save. The Nazirite instructions are specific to Samson and his calling, not a rule for all believers. The abiding truths are God's sovereign grace, His power to give life where there was barrenness, and His faithfulness to raise a deliverer even for an undeserving people.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on four words. The visitor is the malak, the Angel of the LORD, who proves to be God Himself. The child is a nazir, a Nazirite, consecrated to God from the womb. When Manoah asks the Angel's name, He says it is pele, wonderful, beyond comprehension, the very word Isaiah uses of the Messiah. And at the end the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, begins to stir Samson, marking his deliverance as God's own work.
basic.typology: The miraculous birth announced to a barren woman by a heavenly messenger foreshadows the greater annunciations of Scripture, above all the birth of Jesus. Samson, set apart from the womb to begin saving Israel, points forward to Christ, set apart from eternity to accomplish salvation in full. And the Angel who bears the name Wonderful and ascends in the flame anticipates the Son whose name is Wonderful Counselor, and who ascends to the Father.
basic.christ: The Angel of the LORD here receives worship and sacrifice, does wonders, and is identified with God, yet appears in a form men can see and speak with; many read this as a pre-incarnate appearing of the Son. His name, Wonderful, is the name Isaiah gives the coming Messiah, and His ascent in the flame prefigures the ascension of Christ. Samson himself, the promised child, is a shadow of the true Deliverer: announced before his birth, set apart to God, empowered by the Spirit to begin a rescue that only Jesus completes when He crushes the true enemy and saves His people fully and forever.
basic.god: The great actor in this chapter is God. He is patient and gracious, sending help to a people who no longer even ask for it. He is the Lord of life, opening a barren womb. He is holy and awesome, so that seeing Him makes Manoah fear for his life, yet He is merciful, receiving the offering rather than destroying the worshipers. And He is sovereign over salvation, setting apart and empowering a deliverer by His own Spirit, so that the rescue of Israel is unmistakably His doing.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 6 gives the Nazirite vow that shapes Samson's calling. Genesis 18 and Exodus 3 show earlier appearances of the Angel of the LORD identified with God. Luke 1 records the annunciations to Zechariah and to Mary, echoing this barren-womb promise. And Isaiah 9 names the Messiah Wonderful Counselor, the very name the Angel bears here.
basic.oneStory: As the book of Judges descends toward chaos, God's grace shines all the brighter: unasked, He announces and sets apart a deliverer. Yet Samson will only begin the deliverance; his flawed, unfinished rescue leaves the reader longing for the true Nazirite, the Son whose name is Wonderful, who alone saves His people to the end. This birth is one more step in the story that leads to Bethlehem and the manger.

context.literary: Chapter 13 is the overture to the four-chapter Samson cycle, the climactic and most fully developed of the judge narratives, yet uniquely it opens with an annunciation and a birth rather than an oppression and a cry. The absence of any cry for help is pointed: the pattern of the book is breaking down, and God must move first. The chapter is a carefully wrought theophany, with the wife as the perceptive, believing figure and Manoah as the slower, fearful one, building to the awe of the ascending flame and the revelation that they have seen God.
context.historical: The Philistines were a formidable people of the coastal plain, and their forty-year domination pressed hard on Israel, especially the neighboring tribe of Dan around Zorah and Eshtaol. Barrenness was a deep sorrow and social reproach in that culture, which makes the promise all the more gracious. The Nazirite vow, from Numbers 6, normally taken voluntarily for a season, is here imposed by God from the womb and for life, marking Samson as wholly consecrated. Burnt offerings on a rock, apart from a central sanctuary, fit the irregular worship of this unsettled era.
context.canonical: The chapter stands in the long line of God giving sons to barren women, Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, and it directly anticipates the New Testament annunciations of John the Baptist and of Jesus. The Angel of the LORD, worshiped and identified with God yet distinct enough to be seen and addressed, belongs to the thread of Old Testament appearances that Christians have long understood as unveilings of the pre-incarnate Son. Samson's Spirit-empowered, God-initiated deliverance points forward to the Messiah, the true and final Judge and Savior.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God graciously taking the initiative to raise a deliverer in Israel's darkest hour, even without a cry for help, and to unveil, through the awesome visitor whose name is Wonderful, the God who stoops to save an undeserving people and sets apart His deliverer by His own Spirit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's dealings with Manoah's household and the particular Nazirite calling of Samson; it does not prescribe the Nazirite regimen or lifelong abstinence for believers today. What endures is the revelation of God's sovereign, initiating grace, His power over barrenness and death, and His faithfulness to provide a Savior, fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: Who is the Angel of the LORD in this chapter?

views.label: A created angel representing God
views.case: He is called the angel, a messenger, and speaks and acts on God's behalf; on this reading the fear and worship are directed through the messenger to the God who sent him, and the language of seeing God is the awe of encountering His representative.

views.label: A pre-incarnate appearing of the Son
views.case: He accepts sacrifice, does a marvelous wonder, ascends in the flame, bears the wonderful, unsearchable name, and is expressly identified with God so that Manoah says we have seen God; the weight of the chapter presses beyond a mere creature toward a true theophany, which Christians read as the eternal Son.

debates.bearing: Either way the chapter reveals God graciously present to save; the Christophany reading richly connects the visitor's name, Wonderful, and His ascent in the flame to the Lord Jesus.

debates.question: Why does the text stress that this deliverer is set apart from the womb, and that Israel offered no cry for help?

views.label: To magnify God's sheer initiative and grace
views.case: With no cry recorded, God moves unbidden, choosing and consecrating a deliverer before the child even exists; salvation is shown to arise wholly from God's mercy, not from Israel's repentance or request.

views.label: To mark how deep Israel's decline has become
views.case: That the people no longer even cry out shows they have grown numb and comfortable in their bondage; the broken pattern signals a nation so far gone that only God's unprompted grace can begin to rescue it.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on grace: the deeper Israel's numbness, the brighter shines a God who saves before He is asked, foreshadowing the gospel in which Christ comes while we are still helpless.

study.originalLanguages: Four words carry the wonder of the chapter. First, the visitor is the malak, the Angel of the LORD; the word means messenger, but this messenger accepts a burnt offering, works a marvel, and ascends in the altar flame, until Manoah confesses, we have seen God, so that many read Him as the pre-incarnate Son. Second, the child is a nazir, a Nazirite, one separated to God, marked by uncut hair and abstinence from wine and defilement, Numbers 6; uniquely, Samson is consecrated from the womb and for life, his whole existence claimed by God before he draws breath. Third, when Manoah asks His name, the Angel answers that it is pele, wonderful, beyond comprehension, the very word Isaiah 9:6 sets upon the Messiah, Wonderful. And finally the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, begins to stir the boy at Mahaneh-dan; the same Spirit who empowered the earlier judges now takes hold of Samson, marking his coming deliverance as God's own work and not mere human strength. Angel, Nazirite, Wonderful, Spirit: the vocabulary itself preaches that God has drawn near to save.

typology.type: A son promised to a barren woman by a heavenly messenger
typology.fulfillment: The annunciations of John the Baptist and of Jesus, the greater births announced by God's grace, Luke 1:13-17 and Luke 1:30-35.

typology.type: A deliverer set apart to God from the womb
typology.fulfillment: Christ, consecrated from eternity and holy from the womb, set apart to save His people, Luke 1:35 and Hebrews 7:26.

typology.type: The Angel whose name is Wonderful, ascending in the flame
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Wonderful Counselor of Isaiah 9:6, who ascends to the Father, John 20:17 and Acts 1:9.

study.christ: This chapter is full of Christ. The Angel of the LORD receives sacrifice and worship, does a marvelous wonder, is called by a name that is wonderful and beyond comprehension, ascends in the altar flame, and is finally confessed as God Himself, we have seen God; many in the church have rightly seen here a pre-incarnate appearing of the eternal Son, whose name Isaiah gives as Wonderful Counselor, Isaiah 9:6, and who would one day ascend visibly to His Father. The promised child too foreshadows Christ. Samson is announced before his birth to a barren woman, set apart to God from the womb, and empowered by the Spirit to begin Israel's deliverance, just as Jesus is announced by angels, born holy, anointed by the Spirit, and sent to save. Yet Samson only begins the rescue, and begins it flawed; his unfinished, compromised deliverance leaves us aching for the true Nazirite, the sinless Son, who finishes salvation once and for all by crushing the real enemy at the cross.
study.god: God is the true subject of this chapter. He is astonishingly gracious, moving to save a people who no longer even cry out, choosing and consecrating a deliverer before the child is conceived. He is the Lord of life who opens a barren womb, the sovereign who sets apart His servant and stirs him by His Spirit, so that the whole deliverance is plainly His doing. He is holy and awesome, so that to see Him is to fear for one's life, yet He is merciful, receiving the offering and sparing the worshipers, as Manoah's wife wisely discerns. In the visitor whose name is Wonderful, God stoops near enough to be seen and spoken with, revealing a Savior-God who will one day come nearer still, in the flesh, to save His people from their sins.

crossReferences.note: The Nazirite vow of separation to the LORD, uncut hair and abstinence from wine and defilement, that shapes Samson's lifelong consecration.
Earlier appearances of the Angel of the LORD who is identified with God Himself, the thread to which this theophany belongs.
The angelic annunciations of John and of Jesus, the greater births foreshadowed by this promise to a barren woman.
The Messiah named Wonderful Counselor, the same wonderful name the Angel bears in this chapter.

study.oneStory: As Judges spirals toward its darkest chapters, God's grace blazes out: unasked and unmerited, He announces and sets apart a deliverer, sending the Angel whose name is Wonderful to a barren woman and stirring the child by His Spirit. Yet Samson will only begin the deliverance, and even that beginning is marred; his flawed, unfinished rescue keeps the reader longing for the true and final Deliverer. This awe-filled birth belongs to the long line of God-given sons that runs to Bethlehem, to the Son whose name is truly Wonderful, Jesus, who alone saves His people to the very end.

quiz.q: What is striking about how Israel's oppression is described at the start of this chapter?

options.text: Israel does evil and is handed to the Philistines, but no cry for help is recorded; God moves to save unbidden.
options.note: Unlike the earlier cycles, no cry is mentioned, magnifying God's sheer grace in acting first.

options.text: Israel is oppressed by the Philistines for forty years.
options.note: True, but it misses the pointed absence of any cry for rescue that highlights God's initiative.

options.text: Israel repents deeply and begs God for a deliverer.
options.note: The opposite; no repentance or cry is recorded, which is precisely the point.

options.text: Israel defeats the Philistines on its own.
options.note: Israel is under Philistine domination; it does not defeat them here.

quiz.q: What did the Angel of the LORD announce about the coming child?

options.text: He would be a Nazirite to God from the womb and would begin to deliver Israel from the Philistines.
options.note: Verse 5 gives both the lifelong consecration and the mission to begin Israel's deliverance.

options.text: He would be a specially dedicated son given to a barren woman.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits the Nazirite calling and the mission of deliverance.

options.text: He would become a wealthy and powerful ruler.
options.note: The promise concerns consecration and deliverance, not wealth or a throne.

options.text: He would be an ordinary child with no special calling.
options.note: He is set apart as a Nazirite from the womb, anything but ordinary.

quiz.q: How did Manoah and his wife come to realize who their visitor was?

options.text: The Angel did a marvelous thing and ascended in the altar flame, and they fell facedown, saying they had seen God.
options.note: Verses 19-22 record the wonder, the ascent in the flame, and Manoah's cry that they have seen God.

options.text: The visitor told them plainly that he was God.
options.note: He did not give his name; they realized it through the wonder and the ascent in the flame.

options.text: They guessed because he refused their goat.
options.note: His refusal pointed them to worship God, but the revelation came in the ascending flame.

options.text: They never realized who he was.
options.note: Manoah expressly recognized it was the Angel of the LORD and that they had seen God.

quiz.q: What did the Angel say when Manoah asked His name?

options.text: That His name is beyond comprehension, or wonderful, the very word Isaiah uses of the Messiah.
options.note: Verse 18 gives the wonderful, unsearchable name, tied to Isaiah 9:6, Wonderful Counselor.

options.text: That His name was too holy to be spoken aloud.
options.note: Close in spirit, but the text says His name is wonderful and beyond comprehension, not merely unspeakable.

options.text: That His name was Manoah's to choose.
options.note: The Angel keeps His own name hidden; it is not Manoah's to give.

options.text: That His name was simply Gabriel.
options.note: No such name is given; the Angel says His name is wonderful and beyond comprehension.

quiz.q: How does the birth of Samson point forward to Christ?

options.text: A deliverer announced to a barren woman, set apart from the womb, and stirred by the Spirit foreshadows Christ, announced and set apart to save His people fully.
options.note: The annunciation, consecration, and Spirit-anointing all anticipate Jesus, who completes what Samson only begins.

options.text: It shows God gives special children to accomplish His plans.
options.note: True, but it understates how Samson's calling and the Angel named Wonderful point directly to Christ.

options.text: It teaches that deliverers always succeed perfectly.
options.note: Samson only begins the deliverance and does so flawed, which makes us long for the true Savior.

options.text: It has no real connection to Christ.
options.note: The wonderful Angel and the Spirit-set-apart deliverer clearly point to Christ.

questions: God moved to save Israel before they even cried out. How does it change your view of grace to know that God takes the initiative toward people who are not yet asking?
The Angel's name is Wonderful, beyond comprehension. Where do you need to let God be greater and more mysterious than your understanding of Him, and simply worship?
Manoah's wife reasoned that God would not have received their offering only to destroy them. How can you preach that same gospel logic to your own fearful heart when you feel condemned?
Samson was set apart to God from the womb, his whole life claimed for God's purpose. What would it look like to see your own life as wholly consecrated, belonging to the LORD?
Samson only began a deliverance that Christ completes. How does resting in the finished, unflawed salvation of Jesus free you from looking to flawed deliverers, including yourself?

translationNotes.note: Angel may also be rendered Angel with a capital, and the BSB footnote notes that the corresponding pronouns may be capitalized throughout the chapter, reflecting the widespread reading of this figure as a divine appearance.
Nazirite renders a word the BSB footnote also gives as set apart; a Nazirite, from Numbers 6, was consecrated to God, marked by uncut hair and abstinence from wine and defilement.

Beyond comprehension translates a Hebrew word meaning wonderful or full of wonder; it is the same root behind the Messianic title Wonderful in Isaiah 9:6, which is why many hear an unveiling of the coming Christ here.
Mahaneh-dan, where the Spirit began to stir Samson, means camp of Dan, according to the BSB footnote; it lay between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tribe's territory.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 13:1-25. Chapter 13 is prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; only ordinary hyphens in Mahaneh-dan; none of the forbidden dashes anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no verified expository sermon on this specific movement was confirmed this session from the trusted cluster.
